title: Windows MRU folder-access deep dive
action: Pull the user's MRU Folder Access entries and resolve each PIDL GUID to a path. Cross-reference with USB device-arrival timestamps to spot deliberate access to external-drive folders. Tag entries proving intentional dialog navigation; volume letters in MRU entries indicate external drive access.
rationale: MRU Folder Access proves intentional folder navigation through the standard Windows Open/Save dialog — a strong indicator of deliberate user intent (vs system or program-driven access). Vista+ stores PIDL GUIDs that AXIOM and X-Ways both resolve.
expected_result: Path-resolved MRU folder list with timestamps; at least one entry corroborated against a USB or network-share artefact.

axiom.action: AXIOM Examine → Artifacts → MRU/Recent → MRU Folder Access. Sort by Date/Time. Right-click suspicious entries → Add Tag. Use the Connections view to link folder access to USB / network artefacts.
axiom.ui_path: AXIOM Examine > Artifacts > MRU / Recent > MRU Folder Access
axiom.notes: AXIOM presents PIDL GUIDs already resolved; cross-check the resolved path against the USB / Mount-Points artefact for external-drive correlation.

xways.action: In X-Ways, run the MRU registry plugin under Tools → Run X-Ways Forensics Plugin (or a RegRipper-equivalent comdlg32.pl) against the user's NTUSER.DAT in the volume snapshot. Apply column filters on Path / Modified to pull the suspicious window.
xways.ui_path: Tools > Run X-Ways Forensics Plugin (with NTUSER.DAT focused)
xways.notes: X-Ways won't auto-resolve PIDL GUIDs as cleanly as AXIOM; consider running RegRipper externally and importing the output as a logical evidence object for cross-reference.
